participants.reasoning: The extracted participants describe a much older ROADMAP follow-up cohort with different enrollment details, whereas the official study lists younger Israeli participants in a separate trial with different baseline characteristics and eligibility criteria.
participants.extracted: Adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus, aged about 61 years on average, enrolled in the ROADMAP observational follow-up after completing the randomized ROADMAP trial. The follow-up included 1,758 of 2,198 eligible participants: 877 former placebo patients and 881 former olmesartan patients. Participants had been in the original ROADMAP study for about 3.3 years and were generally comparable between groups at follow-up baseline. Most were receiving antihypertensive therapy, and the cohort was representative of the larger ROADMAP population, although not all original trial participants entered the follow-up.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text describes observational follow-up after prior treatment with routine monitoring and physician-discretion therapy, whereas the official intervention specifies a randomized enalapril versus placebo trial with predefined nifedipine rescue criteria.
interventions.extracted: Blood pressure monitoring in the context of follow-up after prior randomized antihypertensive treatment, with patients from the former placebo and olmesartan arms receiving standard medical care and routine blood pressure assessments during the observational follow-up period; this follow-up also recorded any antihypertensive therapy used at the discretion of the primary physician.
interventions.official:
Intervention 1:intensive: enalapril 10 mg daily
Intervention 2:standard: placebo
In either group, if SBP ≥ 145 mmHg or DBP ≥ 95 mmHg on 2 consecutive occasions, long‐acting nifedipine initiated.
Length of follow‐up:
Planned:5 years
Actual:5 years

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes focus mainly on observational follow-up cardiovascular, renal, and mortality endpoints, whereas the official review outcome is diabetic retinopathy incidence/progression assessed yearly by fundoscopy.
outcomes.extracted: The study planned to measure selected former primary and secondary ROADMAP endpoints during observational follow-up, including microalbuminuria incidence/time to onset (assessed by local routine urine albumin testing, including dipstick and UACR >30 mg/g; at follow-up collection points over a mean 3.3 years after the final ROADMAP visit), cardiovascular and cerebrovascular morbidity and mortality (composite and individual events such as acute coronary syndrome, coronary revascularization, silent myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, transient ischemic attack, and stroke; recorded from routine medical care during follow-up up to about 3.3 years), total mortality (number of deaths during follow-up), renal disease/renal function (eGFR/serum creatinine and end-stage renal disease/doubling of creatinine; measured from routine laboratory assessments during follow-up up to about 3.3 years), diabetic retinopathy/new onset diabetic retinopathy (diagnosis from routine ophthalmologic/medical assessment during follow-up up to about 3.3 years), and vital signs including blood pressure, pulse rate, weight, body mass index, and waist circumference (routine clinical measurements at follow-up collection points). Additional laboratory and treatment variables included HbA1c, creatinine/eGFR, and use of antihypertensive and antidiabetic drugs, collected at the two follow-up data collection points occurring a mean of 2.3 years and 3.3 years after the last ROADMAP visit.
outcomes.official:

Secondary outcome, as specified for this review:progression to PDR
Other diabetic retinopathy outcomes:none
Retinopathy diagnosis and monitoring:"Fundoscopy was done yearly by an ophthalmologist, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy was recorded."
